A real fleet is neither single nor shallow — a phantom is inherited again by the next agent, and a fleet branches — so the true quantity the gate protects is the whole set of downstream agents a root corruption reaches. We measured that set live, down a chain and across a fan-out tree, on the same tau2 airline witness with two arms (believe: each agent inherits the corrupt state; adjudicate: the gate blocked the root, so each inherits the gold state). The root corruption is one reservation R wrongly cancelled (injected; every downstream node runs live), and the witness is exact: is R still cancelled when this agent finishes?

Down a single chain the result is linear: under believe a depth-D chain leaves all D−1 downstream nodes corrupt, under adjudicate zero (the gate prevents 1, 2, and 3 corrupt nodes at depths 2, 3, 4). The believe agents are instructive: each, handed a cancelled reservation, simply could not do its task and gave up — none restored R. So a capable peer re-checks and heals a narrated phantom (hence ΔB ≈ 0) but cannot re-check away a corrupt starting world; the state handoff is the one that compounds.

A chain understates a fleet, because fleets branch. A depth-D, fan-out-F dependency tree has FD leaves, all downstream of the root corruption. Under believe every leaf is corrupt (4 of 4 at depth 2, 8 of 8 at depth 3, F = 2); under adjudicate the single root block keeps every leaf clean. So the prevented-corruption payoff is FD — 4 at depth 2, 8 at depth 3 — against the chain’s linear D−1. FD here is breadth (FD agents each blocked by the one shared poison, not one corrupt value mutating into many): a poisoned shared resource fails every agent that depends on it, and that set grows FD as the fleet branches. This is the depth term ΔB pinned to one, now measured live. (Caveats: the root is injected, not waited for, and each node re-inherits the same root state rather than chaining onto the previous node’s output; it is an existence result for the compounding shape — one model, F = 2, depths 2–4 — not a calibrated constant. The fleet-of-one floor holds: at depth 0 the payoff is zero.)

11. References

  1. [1] Barres, V. and Dong, H. and Ray, S. and Si, X. and Narasimhan, K.. $\tau^2$-Bench: Evaluating Conversational Agents in a Dual-Control Environment. arXiv:2506.07982, 2025.
  2. [2] {METR}. Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Software Tasks. arXiv:2503.14499, 2025.
  3. [3] Pathak, D. and others. Detecting Silent Failures in Multi-Agentic AI Trajectories. arXiv:2511.04032, 2025.
  4. [4] Meir, T. (Meirtz). Limen: Coordination for Concurrent Autonomous AI Agents over Shared Mutable State (advisory write-leases over MCP). Software artifact, github.com/Meirtz/Limen, 2025.
  5. [5] Pugachev, S.. CodeCRDT: Observation-Driven Coordination for Multi-Agent LLM Code Generation. arXiv:2510.18893, 2025.
  6. [6] Anderson, J. P.. Computer Security Technology Planning Study. ESD-TR-73-51, U.S. Air Force Electronic Systems Division, 1972.
  7. [7] Wald, A.. Sequential Tests of Statistical Hypotheses. Annals of Mathematical Statistics 16(2):117--186, 1945.
  8. [8] Page, E. S.. Continuous Inspection Schemes. Biometrika 41(1/2):100--115, 1954.
  9. [9] Mohan, C. and Haderle, D. and Lindsay, B. and Pirahesh, H. and Schwarz, P.. ARIES: A Transaction Recovery Method Supporting Fine-Granularity Locking and Partial Rollbacks Using Write-Ahead Logging. ACM Transactions on Database Systems 17(1):94--162, 1992.
  10. [10] Bernstein, P. A. and Hadzilacos, V. and Goodman, N.. Concurrency Control and Recovery in Database Systems. Addison-Wesley, 1987.
  11. [11] Bishop, M. and Dilger, M.. Checking for Race Conditions in File Accesses. Computing Systems 9(2):131--152, 1996.
  12. [12] Lambert, N. and others (Allen Institute for AI). T\"ulu 3: Pushing Frontiers in Open Language Model Post-Training. arXiv:2411.15124, 2024.
  13. [13] Bowman, S. R. and others (Anthropic). Measuring Progress on Scalable Oversight for Large Language Models. arXiv:2211.03540, 2022.
  14. [14] Baker, B. and Huizinga, J. and Gao, L. and others (OpenAI). Monitoring Reasoning Models for Misbehavior and the Risks of Promoting Obfuscation. arXiv:2503.11926, 2025.
